Memorandum

To : Advice Request Meeting Participants                Date : January 12, 1983


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Robert E. Leidigh [handwritten mark]

Subject: Resolution of issues raised in my January 5, 1983 memo
         regarding misconstruction of the PRA, discussed January 6, 1983

         At our meeting we resolved to address these problems in
the following manner:

     1)  Be aggressive in our enforcement and advice policies
         to educate public officials as to the proper inter-
         pretation of the PRA.  This will include proceeding to
         prosecute enforcement actions where reliance on errant
         advice is raised as a defense.  This will be considered
         only for purposes of possible mitigation of cases, not
         as to whether or not a violation has occurred.

     2)  Distribute our conflicts pamphlet as widely as possible
         so that public officials are exposed to the proper
         interpretation of the law.  This will include sending
         copies to city clerks and county clerks for distribution
         to city and county officials as soon as budgetary
         restrictions permit us to make such a distribution.

     3)  [handwritten text - illegible] in CFR publisher &
         [handwritten text - illegible] on a permanent basis to the public.

                                                    Advice
         Approved & [illegible] added at Advice Request Mtg
         1/13/83.

S Orange, Ca
Santa Ynez Valley News
December 2, 1982




TOO STRINGENT

Conflict-of-interest law aired

If the Board of Supervisors insists that a state conflict-of-interest law is too stringent, then they should get the law changed through the legislature.

That is the conclusion of deputy county counsel Jack Cohan in reviewing a state Fair Political Practices Commission ruling that has resulted in the resignation of county Planning Commissioner Dan Lahr of Santa Maria, Santa Barbara Commissioner Peg Hamister selling Exxon stock — at a loss to boot, she said — and the board members wondering how it's going to affect their decisions.

"A reasonable concern exists that the threshold amounts are so unrealistically low as to inhibit qualified persons from accepting public office," stated Cohan in a report to the board Monday.

Although such an amendment would take a two-thirds vote of the legislature, Cohan said an increase in the threshold amounts is "attainable" while still maintaining the integrity of the law.

The state Fair Political Practices Act declares that public officials have an interest if they receive $250 or more per year from a person or firm which is affected by a decision, an investment of $1,000 or more or if they are a director or officer.

Supervisor Bill Wallace, a part-time veterinarian, last week wondered if he'd have a conflict under those rules if a developer paid him more than $250 for his sick dog.

The board did not make any decision on which way to go Monday, as Cohan said more research into the law is ongoing, so he will bring the issue back at a future meeting.

Besides the effects of the FPPC's decision mentioned above, the actions resulted in a delay of the county's reforms of the energy ordinance, designed for more control and review over oil and gas exploration and production onshore, and some deadlocked 2-2 votes on important projects at the commission after Lahr's resignation.

Santa Barbara, CA
News Press
November 16, 1982


CONFLICT-OF-INTEREST CASE

Supervisors want second opinion

The county Board of Supervisors Monday night said it wants a second opinion on a conflict-of-interest allegation centered on the Planning Commission's consideration of a zoning modification for oil and gas facilities.

An attorney with the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) handled that week that Commissioner Peg Hamister of Santa Barbara should disqualify herself from participating in development of the energy ordinances because her husband owns 540 shares of Exxon Corp. common stock valued at some $15,000. (Mrs. Hamster has said her husband will sell the stock.)

The attorney also said that Commissioner John Luhr of Santa Maria may have a conflict because his firm, Luhr Electric Motors, does business with oil companies. She said Luhr would have to make the determination "in good faith."

"Is there any place we can go and ask for another opinion?" Supervisor Robert Kallmann asked deputy county counsel Jack Cohan.

"You could get another opinion from the Attorney General, but he is likely to defer to any opinion from the FPPC," Cohan responded.

The other option, Cohan said, would be for the county to hire a private attorney and seek legal action through a declaratory judgement on the FPPC's opinion.

"But that could be quite expensive," Cohan added, noting that he thought it would be "inappropriate" for the county counsel's office to handle such a case.

Cohan said he was unsure whether the Attorney General would handle any legal action prompted by the FPPC or whether it would be handled by the state commission.

The supervisors were concerned about the effect of the ruling not only on the planning commissions, but also on themselves and members of other commissions.

"You're going to be discouraging and eliminating people in business and professional people from serving on commissions," said Supervisor Harrell Fischer.

"I think there are legal attorneys trying to carry that opinion further than it should be carried," said Supervisor DeWayne Holstrom.

Following about 25 minutes of discussion, the supervisors agreed to schedule the matter for consideration on Monday's regular board agenda.

Although the planner's conflict dominated the discussion, the word was actually meeting Monday night to hold a public hearing on the new zoning code ordinance. But the hearing spanned just 11 minutes and included only two speakers, both of which endorsed the ordinance as currently drafted.

The board continued the hearing until Monday night, when it will take up the new zoning ordinance for the remainder of the county.

—Tom Bolton

Pasadena Star News
December 1, 1982

District attorney takes over probe
of Arcadia mayor

By DAVID WILLMAN
Staff Writer

An inquiry into whether Arcadia Mayor Donald
B. Pellegrino violated state conflict-of-interest law
in connection with a proposed office complex in
the city is now in the hands of the Los Angeles
County District Attorney's office.

Jack Meade, spokesman for the state Fair Political Practices Commission, described it as "fairly
routine" the transfer of the case from his agency
to the district attorney's office.

"They know the players in the game a little
better, and are better able to investigate," Meade
said by phone from Sacramento. "We've offered
to assist them in any capacity."

The state watchdog group, which enforces and
interprets California's conflict-of-interest law,
became involved last month after receiving a
letter from two other Arcadia City Council members, Jeffrey Dring and Richard Haltom.

The pair alleged that Pellegrino violated state
law Oct. 21 when he voted on the proposed office
complex, located across from Santa Anita Park
near the intersection of Colorado Place and
Huntington Drive.

The backers of the project, RPI Services, Inc.,
and Cornerstone Development, were asking the
council, sitting as the city redevelopment agency,
to take a first step toward granting them some $1.5
million in tax-exempt financing.

Such a financing arrangement is possible for the
office complex because it is within Arcadia's redevelopment area. If the council gives final approval
to the tax-exempt-financing plan, it would give the
project's backers money at a fixed, long-term interest rate presumably lower than they could get
from a commercial lender.

California law allows cities to grant tax-exempt
financing to private developers for projects with
potential for public benefit within designated
redevelopment areas.

The possible conflict of interest stemmed, Dring
and Haltom said, from Pellegrino's earlier sale of
a separate piece of Arcadia property to one of the
office project's general partners, David C.
Schultheis, who said he is president of RPI
Services and a 40 percent shareholder in Cornerstone Development.

Schultheis owed Pellegrino and his wife $307,-
500 as of Aug. 31 for the purchase of their property on Alta Street, according to documents
Schultheis provided to the Arcadia council before
the Oct. 21 votes. Those same documents showed

that Schultheis planned to make monthly
payments of $3,331 at an interest rate of 13
percent, with the balance due in March 1986.

For his part, Pellegrino has said that in the
future he won't participate in any council
decisions dealing with Schultheis and the
proposed office complex. Pellegrino noted in an
interview that he voted to support the tax-exempt-
financing arrangement on Oct. 21 only after first
checking with City Attorney Charles Liberto, who
advised him at the meeting that he did not see any
potential conflict of interest.

Asked about his advice, Liberto said that while
he had ordered a property title search on Oct. 15
to confirm that Schultheis was buying the Alta
Street parcel from Pellegrino, it was impossible
for either him or the mayor to know conclusively
of a possible conflict at the time.

"There certainly was a question" of a conflict,
Liberto said, "but there was no definite proof that
this guy (Schultheis), or indication that he had
connections in Cornerstone or RPI at that point.
There was just no connection. "There was a
surprise or a thought. But until we got the property title report (on Nov. 1), we couldn't even
prove that Schultheis owed the mayor" for the
Alta property.

Turn to MAYOR,
Page/A-8

Pasadena Star News
December 1, 1982

Mayor
investigated by district attorney

"We didn't have anything in our hands, in my hands. (Definite . . . that (Schultheis) owed the mayor." Liberto said, adding that since neither he nor Pellegrino actually knew of a potential conflict, there is no possibility of a violation under California law.

"That's the key," Liberto said. "Known, or not known. The (state) Political Reform Act says you've violated nothing if you don't know it's that simple."

Liberto added that he now is advising Pellegrino to abstain from council deliberations on the office project.

Pellegrino has further defended himself by pointing out that he originally sold his Alta Street property to Arcadia resident Gary Weaver, who, as permitted by the conditions of the sale, then nominated Schultheis as the actual purchaser.

Pellegrino also has maintained that Schultheis' monthly checks have gone directly into a savings and loan account and that he did not know who Schultheis was until after the Oct. 21 meeting.

And Liberto has acknowledged that his advice to Pellegrino at the Oct. 21 meeting could ultimately shield the mayor from any potential enforcement penalty.

"My ruling, in some cases, yes, could protect

him from a type of liability exposure and, in some instances, no," said Liberto, who announced in September he would resign his post after the council selects a successor.

Meanwhile, Dona Bracke, the deputy district attorney assigned to the Pellegrino case, said it probably will be a year before the matter is resolved due to the heavy backlog of cases in the Special Investigations Unit.

"I have about 40 cases pending right now," Ms. Bracke said.

Los Angeles Times
December 5, 1982

Arcadia

Complaint Against
Mayor Transferred

The state Fair Political Practices Commission has
transferred a conflict of interest complaint against
Arcadia Mayor Donald D. Pellegrino to the county
District Attorney's Office.

Commission spokesman John Meade said the transfer
was routine in such cases. Councilmen Richard A.
Haltom and Jeffrey A. Dring have alleged Pellegrino
violated the Political Reform Act of 1974 when he voted
on a matter involving a developer's request to use
tax-exempt industrial revenue bonds to finance an office
building. One of the partner companies connected to the
development firm owes Pellegrino money.

Pellegrino acknowledged the developer owed him the
money, but denied he had a conflict. Joe DiVirgilio, a
spokesman for the District Attorney's Special Investiga-
tions Unit, said it would take several months before
the case is resolved because his office has a backlog of
more than 50 cases.

Los Angeles Times
November 14, 1982

Mayor Accused of
Interest Conflict

2 Arcadia Councilmen Complain
to Commission After Bond Vote

By ALAN MALTUN, Times Staff Writer

ARCADIA—Allegations that Mayor Donald D. Pellegrino had a conflict of interest when he voted on a bond issue for a proposed office building have brought a complaint to the state Fair Political Practices Commission.

City Councilmen Richard A. Haltom and Jeffrey A. Dring accused Pellegrino of violating the Political Reform Act of 1974 by voting on matters involving a tax-exempt industrial revenue bond issue requested by a partnership which includes a San Carlos firm that owes Pellegrino more than $300,000.

Pellegrino acknowledged he holds a mortgage on property owned by the development company, but said it was a coincidence, which emerged about after his initial vote on the bond request. He denied any conflict existed and said that once he became aware of the connection, he voted on the bond request only after City Atty. Charles Liberto advised him he had no conflict. Dring and Haltom's charges are politically motivated, Pellegrino said.

Mayor Will Abstain

"I have not voted on anything that was not sanctioned by our city attorney," he declared. "There would publicly embarrass somebody and privately apologize."

Pellegrino, however, said he would abstain from voting on matters involving the company's tax-exempt financing in the future. Final action on the firm's bond request is expected by the council in three weeks.

The development company, Colorado Partners Ltd., has asked the city to issue $1.6 million in industrial development bonds to finance an office building at Colorado Boulevard and Colorado Freeway. Colorado Partners Ltd. actually is a partnership consisting of RPI Services Inc. and Cornerstone Development.

The possible conflict of interest involves Cornerstone Development, which owes Pellegrino $307,500 on three residential lots he once owned on Alta Street.

Dring and Haltom said they filed the FPPC complaint because they disagreed with Liberto's opinion that no conflict existed and could not dissuade Pellegrino from continuing to vote on the bond request.

Please see CONFLICT, Page 3

CONFLICT: Councilmen
Charge Mayor Erred in Vote
on Bonds

"I thought he had a conflict," Dring said. "It appears that in these hard times when virtually no property is selling, he turned around and sold them $300,000 worth of property, then they asked us for (approval of) $1.6 million in tax exempt bonds. To me that's not arm's length. It just doesn't look healthy and I think it should be investigated."

Haltom conceded that he and Dring frequently are less supportive of redevelopment projects than Pellegrino, but insisted. "The allegations aren't politically motivated. I would have Dring investigated (under the same circumstances)."

The connection between Pellegrino and Cornerstone Development has a convoluted, if confusing past. Pellegrino, stung by charges of a conflict of interest two years ago when he voted to approve a hotel in the redevelopment zone near three residential lots he owns on Alta Street, said he decided to sell the parcels to avoid future problems.

He said he sold the lots for $367,500 to Gary Weaver, but Weaver apparently could not complete the transaction, so Weaver's friend, David Schultheis, took over the property in escrow.

Schultheis, a general partner in RPI Services Inc., Cornerstone Development and Colorado Partners Ltd., said he took title to the property and assumed the $3,331 monthly payments.

Pellegrino said he did not know of Schultheis' connection with the three firms, apparently because Schultheis' name did not appear on documents they filed with the city in connection with the office project. Another executive with the firms handled dealings with the Arcadia Redevelopment Agency and city staff.

Cornerstone Development last August, and when the city asked to see a financial statement before it voted on tax exempt financing for the office project, Pellegrino's name appeared as the holder of a $307,500 mortgage on the Cornerstone-owned property.

Pellegrino 'Unaware'

Pellegrino said he was not aware of his connection with Cornerstone or Schultheis' relationship with the company until the financial statement reached the City Council in early October.

"None of us was aware of the (mortgage) note was in there until we asked for a financial statement," Pellegrino said. Pellegrino and Schultheis said they

never even met each other until last Monday.
The votes involving the bond reparat have occurred
at meetings of the council and the Arcadia Redevelop-
ment Agency, whose members are the same. In
September, the agency and the council voted 4-0 to
tentatively approve the bond idea. Councilmen Dennis
Lojeski and David Hannah joined Pellegrino and Haltom
on the vote. Dring was absent.
On Oct. 21, about two weeks after the financial
statement arrived at City Hall, Dring raised the conflict
of interest issue at a council meeting and called for
reconsideration of the original resolution tentatively
approving tax-exempt financing for the Colorado P-rt-
ners project. He and Haltom wanted to rescind the
resolution because of what they view as a possi-
ble conflict of interest by Pellegrino in earlier votes on the
matter and because they became concerned about
possible liability if the bonds defaulted.
The vote on the reconsideration motion was tied 2 to 2
and would have died without a third vote against it. But,
after being advised by the city attorney that no conflict
existed, Pellegrino joined Hannah and Lojeski to vote
against the motion.
Dring and Haltom disagreed with Liberto's opinion


L.A. Times
November 14, 1982
Continued


CONFLICT: Mayor Accused After Vote on Bonds

Continued from Third Page

and filed their complaint with the Fair Political
Practices Commission about a week later. An FPPC
spokesman said the complaint is being reviewed, but he
could not say when or if an investigation would take
place. If the agency determines that a conflict exists,
Pellegrino could face a fine of up to $2,000 a violation.

Bond Plan in Jeopardy

Pellegrino has his reputation and possibly his political
career on the line, but he is not the only one with
problems. It appears unlikely that the council will
approve the bond financing plan. With Pellegrino
abstaining, the vote for approval probably will be 2-2,
which would kill the proposal.
Although technically the city would issue the bonds,
the developer would be responsible for paying them off,
according to Dring. On the city's bond consultant. The
city would not be liable should the developer default on
the bonds, which yield interest exempt from federal and
state taxes, he said.
"The only thing the city is doing is qualifying us for
tax-exempt financing," said Paul Schultheis, a partner
in the project and David Schultheis' brother. "I still have
to go out and find the money."
Paul Schultheis said his company probably will hold

the Colorado property until interest rates are lower if it
cannot get bond financing.
This is the first time Arcadia has considered issuing
industrial development bonds, Gunn said.
Meanwhile, Dring and Haltom, who were unsuccess-
ful in efforts to get more allies on the council in April's
municipal election, have their credibility on the line.
Councilmen Lojeski and Hannah, who generally are
political allies of Pellegrino, contend that the mayor is
an honest and sincere man who was only following the
advice of the city attorney.
"A more honest individual you're never going to meet
than Don Pellegrino," Lojeski said, adding that he was
more concerned with moving the office building project
along than with his colleagues' accusations.
"I felt comfortable with the city attorney's decision
and I didn't think it was proper or right to question him.
The bottom line is I want to see these go. I don't
care where they get their money.
Hannah said, "It [the charges] is a difficult question
and I feel it should be left up to the authorities.
Pellegrino is a sincere individual who would not
intentionally violate any code of ethics."
They would not comment directly on the political
ramifications of the controversy, but Lojeski said, "In
the couple of years I've known both of these individuals
(Dring and Haltom), I've never attempted to guess
anything (about their motivations)."

Covina Tribune
November 5, 1982

Complaint Hits Arcadia Mayor
Councilmen Accuse Him of $307,500 Interest Conflict in Sale of Property

By STEVE PETERANDT
Staff Writer

Two Arcadia councilmen have filed a complaint with the state Fair Political Practices Commission accusing Mayor Thomas Pellegrino of a $307,500 conflict of interest.

In an Oct. 28 letter to the FPPC, councilmen Jeffrey Dring and Richard Halton alleged that Pellegrino violated the Political Reform Act of 1974 by voting on the proposed RPI Inc. office project at Foothill Boulevard and Colorado Place.

"We believe that Pellegrino has sold some property to an individual, or individuals, who have a proprietary interest in a company known as RPI Development and or Cornerstone Development," Dring and Halton said in their letter.

"Pellegrino has voted repeatedly to proceed in a manner that will provide the project with tax-exempt financing even though the developer owes (him) more than $300,000," they alleged.

Dring and Halton said Pellegrino voted favorably on RPI's request knowing that tax-exempt financing would save the developer enough in interest charges to cover his personal debt.

According to a certified public accountant's report of RPI's finances, Cornerstone Development — the development arm of RPI Inc. — owes Pellegrino and his wife, Yolanda, $307,500 as of Aug. 31. The report indicated Cornerstone is repaying Pellegrino's $3,331 per month at 13 percent annual interest with principal due on March 18, 1988.

Pellegrino said Tuesday he is still not convinced any RPI officers bought his property. He said he sold the land in 1979 to an investor who was later unable to make the payments. Payments eventually were taken over by businessman David Shuttheis, he said. Shuttheis is listed on RPI's financial documents as a partner in Cornerstone Development.

The city attorney is looking at my papers right now," Pellegrino said Tuesday. "He's going to find out if Shuttheis is connected with (RPI)."

Pellegrino said he would abstain from further voting on the RPI project if it turns out that Shuttheis is directly involved in the office project.

City Attorney Charles Liberto ruled two weeks ago that Pellegrino did not have a conflict. He said RPI's financial statement was incorrect in stating that Cornerstone owed Pellegrino $307,500. Liberto said Pellegrino sold his land to a private individual, not to the corporate entity.

But Dring said later he was not satisfied with Liberto's opinion. "How close does the relationship have to be before there's a conflict?" he said. "It's the same assets." John Meade, special assistant to the FPPC, said the law requires public officials to abstain from voting on any matter that would affect a personal financial interest of $250 or more.

"If someone owes someone else money for the sale of property, it would be a source of income to the person and could quite conceivably be a conflict situation," he said.

The FPPC could treat the complaint as an administrative matter or turn it over to the district attorney for criminal investigation, Meade said. If the FPPC finds that a conflict exists, it could also fine Pellegrino up to $2,000 for each violation.

Marysville, CA
Appeal-Democrat
October 23, 1982

Sutter's Proposition
A

Conflict Charge; Buttes Area
Zoning Campaign Heats Up

By ERIC VODDEN
A-D Staff Writer

Charges this week that Sutter
County Supervisor Joe Benatar had a
conflict of interest in voting to support Sutter County's Proposition A—
charges Benatar vigorously denies—
was the first public sign of any opposition to the initiative.

Until this week, outspoken opponents of the proposed construction of
a tomato processing plant that could
be built if the proposition passes have
been mysteriously quiet since supervisors placed the initiative on the ballot last July.

But the conflict-of-interest charge
may be a sign that campaigning on
the measure — with only slightly
more than a week until the election—
may be heating up.

Proposition A will ask voters on
Nov. 2 to rezone about 1,800 acres
west of Sutter — including about 300-
acres between Highway 20 and South
Butte Road that would be designated
"a 'food processing zone.'" That zone
would allow the tomato and other
processing plants.

Should voters give the go-ahead for
the zone, already lengthy legal proceedings surrounding the proposed
tomato plant would be sidestepped.

Voters will not even find an argument against the proposition on sample ballots next to the argument in
support of the measure signed by Sutter County supervisors Roger Chandler, Richard Withrow, Allen Eager
and Benatar.

The only public sign of opposition
— other than a smattering of letters
to the editor — came this week in the
form of the conflict-of-interest
charge filed with the state Fair Political Practices Commission by a
longtime opponent of the tomato
plant.

That complaint reportedly maintains Benatar had a conflict in voting
to support Proposition A when the

Cont. on Back Page ♦

Appeal-Democrat
October 23, 1982
Page Two

Proposition A: Buttes Zoning
Campaign Heats Up

♦ Cont. from Front page
company to is employed by (Western
Title Insurance Co.) owned a major-
ity of the property within the prop-
osed zone before it was transferred
back to Sutter Foods Inc. who have
proposed the processing plant.

Benatar, who is the manager of
Western Title Insurance Co. offices
in Yuba City and Marysville, vehe-
mently denies the allegation, saying
Western Title only had a holding
agreement and never owned any por-
tion of the property.

Furthermore, Benatar maintains
he was cleared of a possible conflict
of interest in 1979 by County Council
Darrell Larsen when Benatar him-
self inquired about a possible conflict
in the matter.

Supervisors placed the matter on
the ballot after petitions bearing
4,000 signatures were turned in last
June to qualify the measure. The
body could have either placed the
matter on the ballot or approved its
provisions outright.

The initiative would create a new
"food processing zone" and allow the
construction of the tomato plant
along Morehead Road. Any proces-
sing plants would be limited to the
area between Highway 20 and South
Butte Road.

The acreage not included in the
"food processing" zone and extend-
ing north to the base of the Sutter
Buttes would be designated for "re-
creational purposes, including golf
courses. Waste water disposal
through irrigation would also be
allowed on the remainder of the prop-
erty.

Initially Sutter Foods Inc. publi-
cized ambitious plans for a golf
course, recreation area and housing
development to be built north of the
agricultural industrial park. Though
immediate plans for such a develop-
ment have been pushed into the back-
ground, the proposition would still al-
ter zoning to allow such a project.

If Proposition A is approved, the
tomato plant could be built "as a mat-

ter of right" without the use permit
that was granted by supervisors in
1978.

The project was initially delayed
by a lawsuit filed by a small group of
Sutter residents formerly called Sut-
ter Sensible Planners which opposed
the project.

Nevis Industries, the owners of the
property, and Sutter Foods Inc., be-
gan circulating petitions early this
year to get the matter before the vo-
ters.

Proponents of the initiative cite the
need for such processing plants to
support area agriculture and to provi-
de jobs in a high unemployment
area.

Opponents to the tomato plant have
claimed there are inadequate provi-
sions for the disposal of agricultural
waste, that such processing plants
would be harmful to the environment
of the Sutter Buttes and that resi-
dents of Sutter will be subjected to
neighborhood deterioration. Visual
blight and adverse environmental
impacts.

After supervisors approved the use
permit for the plant, Sutter Sensible
Planners filed a lawsuit against the
county, claiming an accompanying
environmental impact report (EIR)
on the proposed project was inade-
quate.

Although a subsequent Sutter
County Superior Court ruling upheld
the supervisors approval of the per-
mit, the matter was tied up in state
appeals courts until September 1980.

The Third District Court of Appeal
then ruled there were inadequacies
in the way the EIR was circulated. It
ordered the document recirculated
and directed supervisors to act on it
again.

Instead, Sutter Foods Inc. decided
to place the matter before the voters.

The conflict-of-interest complaint
filed by Russell Mayfield of Sutter
has been received by the state Fair
Political Practices Commission, a
spokesman for the commission con-
firmed.

But Robert Stern of the commis-
sion would not say whether his office

will actually pursue the accusation
beyond an initial evaluation.

The complaint centers on deeds
transferring 1,100 acres of the prop-
erty from Nevis Industries to West-
ern Title, which in turn transferred
the property to Sutter Foods Inc.

Mayfield maintains Benatar voted
to approve Proposition A while his company
"owned a majority of property
within the zone" as of July 27, the
same date Benatar and the rest of the
board voted to place the matter on
the ballot.

Benatar said he will, at Tuesday's
regular board meeting, demand the
Fair Political Practices Commission
conduct an investigation so, "I can be
vindicated."

He notes there was a "holding
agreement" between Nevis and Sut-
ter Foods specifying that Western Ti-
tle hold the property before it was
transferred. That did not designate
ownership of the property, Benatar
said. At all times Sutter Foods was

the legal owner of the property, he
added.

"I am willing to sign an affidavit
under penalty of perjury that West-
ern Title has never had any financial
interest in the property in question,"
Benatar said.

He noted that in 1979 he requested
an opinion from Larsen of whether
his employment by Western Title
could be construed as a conflict of
interest.

Because applicants before the
board have only been customers of
Western Title and, because Benatar
has no ownership interest in the com-
pany, there is no conflict, according
to a letter signed by Larsen in 1979.

Larsen's letter was addressed to
the Fair Political Practices Commis-
sion — bearing out Benatar's claim
the matter was brought to the atten-
tion of the commission at that time.
Sutter County itself does not em-
ploy Western Title Insurance its
transactions because of Benatar's
connection.

Yuba City Valley Herald
October 27, 1982

Correction

In last week's issue a story on alleged conflict of interest charges filed against Sutter County Supervisor Joe Benatar incorrectly stated that Benatar was "under investigation" by the State Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC).

Technically the FPPC is "evaluating," not, "investigating" the charges filed against Supervisor Benatar.

Evaluation is a routine FPPC first step when charges are filed, a step that either precedes a formal in-

vestigation or indicates the charges are baseless or filed with the wrong agency, and thus not deserving of a formal FPPC investigation.

Since there is a technical difference between an "evaluation" and a formal "investigation," and since technicalities can make the difference between charges being evaluated or investigated, between guilt or innocence, we acknowledge our technical misstatement.

The Valley Herald regrets the error.

State of California

Memorandum

March 3, 1983

TO: Ted, Barbara, Bob S., Bob B., Janis, Judy

FROM: Bob Leideigh [?]

SUBJECT: Recurring Misconstruction of Material Financial
Effects On....

As you will recall, at recent advice request meetings (1/5/83
and 1/13/83) we discussed the problem of agency counsel misconst-
ruing our advice and the Act with respect to the application of
87103. I cited four examples at the time. After discussion, it
was determined that we should adopt a more "aggressive" policy
in "getting the word out". (See M-83025) Recently, I have had
two additional instances of this.

1) On February 15, 1983, I received a phone call from Don
Lindberg, City Attorney of Chula Vista. In the course of that
discussion he articulated the belief that the effect of the
decision had to be on the income of the official rather than on
the source of that income. He further stated that he had developed
this belief in "discussions" with other city attorneys. When I
cited Witt v. Morrow to him he debated with me if's holding, so
was both aware of it and had read it. Subsequent to our conver-
sation he apparently changed the advice which he was giving to a
councilmember (although he has apparently misstated another
aspect of our conversation, which I will rectify). In any event,
his misapplication of the Act may have led to a violation by his
client.

2) On March 1, 1983, I received a phone call from Mike Miller,
City Attorney of Arcadia. In the course of that discussion,
Mr. Miller also articulated the belief that, since his client's
income level was fixed by long-term leases, there could be no
material financial effect. I pointed out that this was not the
case and that the effect on his sources of income would have to
be considered. He expressed serious reservations over this and
wanted to know how I concluded this was so. I explained to him
our application of 87103 and cited Witt v. Morrow again. He
indicated that he had read Witt v. Morrow, but read it differently.
I believe that I finally convinced him of the correctness of our
interpretation.

Memo to:  Ted, Barbara, Bob S., Bob B.,
            Janis, Judy
March 3, 1983
Page Two

     Mr. Miller raised another issue during our discussion.  He
asked if we had difficulty in getting officials to request FPPC
advice.  He felt that there might be a reluctance to do so because
a letter from us requiring disqualification would be looked upon
as a finding of a conflict of interest.  I explained that a
conflict of interest only occurs if someone acted when they had
a financial interest rather than disqualify themselves. I
explained that, in fact, by seeking our advice, an official
is doing exactly what the law wants, not doing something illegal.

     The conversation raised some thoughts in my mind which I
wanted to share with you.

     The term "conflict of interest" is not defined in the Act.
It is used in three places - the title of Chapter 7 and with
respect to Conflict of Interest Codes and in Section 87102(c).
In the last, it is used in its proper context.  87100 and 87103
speak in terms of financial interests.  People file Statements of
Economic Interests.  Perhaps we should call codes "Financial
Interests Codes" and should be careful in our advice letters
and opinions to speak only in terms of financial interests and
disqualification.  "Conflict of Interest" should be left to
Enforcement for use when violations have occurred.

Your Thoughts?

BL:ms

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 927 • SACRAMENTO, CA 95814 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
TELEPHONE • • • ALICE TESNEAU • • • DOUGLAS CASEY • • • ENFORCEMENT • • • DIRECTOR OF ENFORCEMENT
916-323-3656        479-5900            322-5691            916-5814        322-6144

RECEIVED JAN 13, 1979

Robert L. Leslie
418 Chiyavera Way
Sonora, CA 95370

Dear Mr. Leslie:

The purpose of this letter is to confirm in writing the
advice given to you in our telephone conversation to your letter
dated January 30, 1979.

The facts, as presented in our letter, were that a
decision is currently pending by the City of Sonora whether to
rezone property which is contiguous to your personal residence.
Assuming you are disqualified from participating in the rezoning
decisions affecting this parcel of property, you asked of
the following: series of questions: May you participate in the
rezoning decisions in your private capacity; if there is a tie
vote, may you cast the deciding vote; and finally to what degree
of accountability will you be held by relying on advice rendered
by the city attorney which was later found to be in error.

With respect to your first question, I pointed out during
our telephone conversations of February 2 and 9, 1979, that you
may participate in the rezoning decision in your private capacity
pursuant to Commission Regulation 18700(d)(2). An official is
prohibited by the Political Reform Act from participating in a
governmental decision in which he or she has a financial interest
provided, however that,

Appearances by a public official as a member of the
general public before a agency in the course of its
prescribed governmental function to represent himself
or herself on matters related solely to his or her
personal interest,

Cal. Admin. Code 18700(d)(2)

are permitted. You indicated during our conversation that you

Robert L. Leake
February 13, 1970

Page Two

might become organized for a group of members in the
affected area. I informed you that such activity generally
would be contrary to the spirit of the Resolution since
you would not be in a position within which you are interested. IF
you wish to pursue this question further, you should outline
in writing the specific governmental deviation involved, the
nature of your group's interest and the nature of the group
you wish to represent. Based on this information we will
provide you with additional guidance.

Government Code Section 3101 of the Act addresses your
second question about passive non-breaking votes. The Act
is rather specific in that an individual who was previously
disqualified cannot return for the purpose of casting a
tie-breaking vote. Section 3101 provides,

Section 3710( [illegible] disqualification provision)
does not prevent any public official from making
or participating in the making of a governmental
decision to the extent his participation is
legally required for the action or decision to
be made. The fact that an official is
needed to break a tie does not make his partic-
ipation legally required for purpose of this
section.

(Emphasis added)

Therefore, you may not participate in the rescinded decision for
the purpose of breaking a tie to ratified.

Finally, you are advised that we are not informed by
relying on advice given by which attorney is found
found to be incorrect. I feel certain that if no advice received
from the city attorney was sound and complied with in good faith
our office would not take any enforcement action from you.
As a practical matter, it would be unlikely that any other person
would succeed in an enforcement action under these circumstances.
However, nothing in the foregoing should deter you from filing of such
a suit.

I hope that our decision has been helpful. If we can be of
further assistance regarding this matter, please do not hesitate
to contact our office.

Sincerely,

[signature]

David Dickerson
Local Counsel
Districts Turnpike Division

embedding.qa_text: Memorandum To: Advice Request Meeting Participants Date: January 12, 1983 From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Robert E. Leidigh Subject: Resolution of issues raised in my January 5, 1983 memo regarding misconstruction of the PRA, discussed January 6, 1983 At our meeting we resolved to address these problems in the following manner: 1) Be aggressive in our enforcement and advice policies to educate public officials as to the proper interpretation of the PRA. This will include proceeding to prosecute enforcement actions where reliance on errant advice is raised as a defense. This will be considered only for purposes of possible mitigation of fines, not as to whether or not a violation has occurred. 2) Distribute our conflicts pamphlet as widely as possible so that public officials are exposed to the proper interpretation of the law. This will include sending copies to city clerks and county clerks for distribution to city and county officials as soon as state budgetary restrictions permit us to make such a distribution. 3) Publish enforcement decisions in CFB publication so they are available on a permanent basis to the public. Approved w/ #3 added at Advise Request Mtg 1/13/83 TOO STRINGENT Conflict-of-interest law aired If the Board of Supervisors insists that a state conflict-of-interest law is too stringent, then they should get the law changed through the legislature. That is the conclusion of deputy county counsel Jack Cohan in reviewing a state Fair Political Practices Commission ruling that has resulted in the resignation of county Planning Commissioner Dan Lahr of Santa Maria, Santa Barbara Commissioner Peg Hamister selling Exxon stock — at a loss to boot, she said — and the board members wondering how it's going to affect their decisions. "A reasonable concern exists that the threshold amounts are so unrealistically low as to inhibit qualified persons from accepting public office," stated Cohan in a report to the board Monday. Although such an amendment would take a two-thirds vote of the legislature, Cohan said an increase in the threshold amounts is "attainable" while still maintaining the integrity of the law. The state Fair Political Practices Act declares that public officials have an interest if they receive $250 or more per year from a person or firm which is affected by a decision, an investment of $1,000 or more or if they are a director or officer. Supervisor Bill Wallace, a part-time veterinarian, last week wondered if he'd have a conflict under those rules if a developer paid him more than $250 for his sick dog. The board did not make any decision on which way to go Monday, as Cohan said more research into the law is ongoing, so he will bring the issue back at a future meeting. Besides the effects of the FPPC's decision mentioned above, the advice has resulted in a delay of the county's reforms of the energy ordinance, designed for more control and review over oil and gas exploration and production onshore, and some deadlocked 2-2 votes on important projects at

S Orange, Ca Santa Ynez Valley News December 2, 1982 TOO STRINGENT Conflict-of-interest law aired If the Board of Supervisors insists that a state conflict-of-interest law is too stringent, then they should get the law changed through the legislature. That is the conclusion of deputy county counsel Jack Cohan in reviewing a state Fair Political Practices Commission ruling that has resulted in the resignation of county Planning Commissioner Dan Lahr of Santa Maria, Santa Barbara Commissioner Peg Hamister selling Exxon stock — at a loss to boot, she said — and the board members wondering how it's going to affect their decisions. "A reasonable concern exists that the threshold amounts are so unrealistically low as to inhibit qualified persons from accepting public office," stated Cohan in a report to the board Monday. Although such an amendment would take a two-thirds vote of the legislature, Cohan said an increase in the threshold amounts is "attainable" while still maintaining the integrity of the law. The state Fair Political Practices Act declares that public officials have an interest if they receive $250 or more per year from a person or firm which is affected by a decision, an investment of $1,000 or more or if they are a director or officer. Supervisor Bill Wallace, a part-time veterinarian, last week wondered if he'd have a conflict under those rules if a developer paid him more than $250 for his sick dog. The board did not make any decision on which way to go Monday, as Cohan said more research into the law is ongoing, so he will bring the issue back at a future meeting.
